headline: Asian Cities Rise as Europe's Liveability Stalls
meta_description: Asian cities are climbing the Global Liveability Index while European scores stagnate. Nine Asian cities now rank in the top 20.
summary_short: The latest Global Liveability Index reveals a shift: Asian cities are rising while Western Europe's scores plateau. Copenhagen still leads.
body:
Copenhagen has reclaimed the world's most liveable city title for another year, but the real story lies in what's happening elsewhere on the rankings.

While the Danish capital scored perfect marks for education, infrastructure and stability, the index reveals a dramatic regional shift. Asian cities now occupy nine of the top 20 spots—up significantly from previous years—while European representation has dropped to seven cities.

China's industrial cities are driving much of this upward movement. Fuzhou, Wuxi, Nanjing, Zhuhai, and Qingdao all jumped the rankings following years of substantial public investment in healthcare. The improvements are striking: Chinese cities dominate the list of biggest movers this year.

Japan has two cities in the top 10, with Osaka at seventh and Tokyo at tenth. Australia maintains three representatives: Melbourne, Sydney, and Adelaide. Meanwhile, Western Europe's average liveability score has stagnated, even as individual cities like Vienna hold their ground at second place.

The index assessed 173 cities across stability, healthcare, culture, environment, education, and infrastructure. It also captured signs of recovery in conflict-affected regions—Damascus's healthcare scores improved markedly following recent political change, offering glimmers of hope even at the rankings' bottom.
